subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 14! In this unit you will master two of the most useful measurement skills in all of mathematics: perimeter and area. Perimeter tells you the distance all the way around the edge of a shape. Area tells you how much flat space is inside a shape. These two ideas look similar at first, but they answer completely different questions, and knowing which one you need is a skill you will use for the rest of your life.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is written for self-paced learning. That means you set the speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto your own paper and work it through yourself, and only move on once the idea feels solid. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you more practice, to explain a step in a fresh way, or to check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels difficult.

Keep a pencil, a ruler and some squared paper beside you as you work. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the shapes, label the sides, and say each formula out loud as you use it. When you make a mistake, do not worry; a mistake is simply a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Every skill in this unit becomes automatic with a little honest practice, and the confidence you build here will carry you into area of triangles, surface area and volume in the years ahead.
Explain the difference between perimeter (distance around) and area (space inside).
Measure and calculate the perimeter of rectangles, squares, triangles and irregular shapes.
Calculate the area of rectangles and squares using length × width.
Calculate the area of a triangle using ½ × base × height.
Find the area and perimeter of compound shapes by splitting or subtracting rectangles.
Estimate the area of an irregular shape by counting whole and part squares.
Choose sensible units and convert between them (cm ↔ m, cm² ↔ m²).
Solve real-world problems involving cost, materials, fencing and flooring.

concepts.title: Understanding Perimeter
concepts.explanation: Perimeter is the distance all the way around the outside edge of a shape. Picture walking right around the very edge of a football pitch until you arrive back where you started – the total distance you walked is the perimeter. If you were building a fence around a field, the perimeter tells you exactly how much fencing to buy. Because perimeter is a length, we always measure it in units of length: millimetres (mm), centimetres (cm), metres (m) or kilometres (km). Never in square units – those are only for area.…
concepts.example: To find the perimeter of any shape, you simply add up the lengths of all its sides. That rule never fails, and it works for every shape, no matter how strange. For common shapes, though, we have quicker methods. For a…

concepts.title: Understanding Area
concepts.explanation: Area is the amount of flat space inside a shape. Think of how much carpet you would need to cover a floor completely, or how much paint to cover a wall. That covering amount is the area. Where perimeter is about the border, area is about the whole surface inside. Because area measures a flat surface, we always measure it in square units: square centimetres (cm²), square metres (m²) or square kilometres (km²). A square unit is simply a square that is one unit long on each side. So 1 cm² is a square measuring 1 cm…
concepts.example: Why does length × width give the area? Imagine covering a rectangle with 1 cm squares. If the rectangle is 6 cm long and 4 cm wide, you can fit 6 squares along each row, and there are 4 rows. That is 6 × 4 = 24 little…

concepts.title: Rectangles and Squares – Putting the Formulas to Work
concepts.explanation: Rectangles and squares appear everywhere, from a sheet of paper to a football pitch to a floor tile, so being quick and confident with their formulas is a huge advantage. Let us gather the four key formulas in one place and then practise using them carefully. Rectangle perimeter: P = 2 × (length + width)
concepts.example: Here is the golden habit that keeps you from muddling area and perimeter: before you calculate anything, ask yourself, *am I going around the edge or covering the inside?* If you are going around, you are finding…

concepts.title: Area of a Triangle
concepts.explanation: Triangles are the next shape to master, and they hide a lovely secret. Take any rectangle and cut it in half from one corner to the opposite corner. You get two matching triangles, and each triangle is exactly half of the rectangle. That is why the area of a triangle is half the area of the rectangle that would fit around it. The formula is Area = ½ × base × height. Two words need care here. The base is the side you choose to measure along the bottom. The height is the straight up-and-down distance from that base…
concepts.example: Let us work an example. A triangular sail on a small model boat has a base of 8 cm and a height of 6 cm. Area = ½ × 8 × 6. A tidy way to calculate this is to multiply the base and height first, 8 × 6 = 48, and then take…

concepts.title: Compound Shapes
concepts.explanation: Most real shapes are not simple rectangles. A house floor plan, an L-shaped garden, or a swimming pool with a shallow end is a compound shape – a shape made by joining two or more simple shapes together. The good news is that you already have all the tools you need. The trick is to break the compound shape into simple rectangles, work out each one, and then combine them. There are two methods for finding the area of a compound shape, and both are worth knowing. The addition method: split the shape into two or more…
concepts.example: Let us use a real L-shaped garden. Imagine the garden is like a large 6 m by 5 m rectangle with a 2 m by 2 m square bite taken out of one corner. Using the subtraction method: the big rectangle is 6 × 5 = 30 m², and the…

concepts.title: Estimating the Area of Irregular Shapes
concepts.explanation: Not every shape is made of neat rectangles and triangles. The outline of a lake, a leaf, your own hand, or a country on a map is an irregular shape with curved or crooked edges. We cannot use a simple formula for these, but we can estimate the area very well by counting squares. The method is called counting squares. Place the shape on a grid of squares, where each square is a known size such as 1 cm². Then count in two steps. First, count all the whole squares that lie completely inside the shape. Second, look at…
concepts.example: For example, suppose a leaf is drawn on centimetre-squared paper. You count 18 whole squares fully inside the leaf. Around the curved edge you find several part squares; judging them, about 7 of them are half-or-more…

methods.title: How to solve any area or perimeter problem
methods.example: A rectangular garden is 12 m long and 9 m wide. Find its perimeter.
steps: Read: decide whether the problem needs area (covering inside) or perimeter (around the edge).
Write: write the correct formula for the shape.
Substitute: put in the numbers with their units and calculate.

methods.title: How to find the area of a compound shape
methods.example: Find the area of the same 12 m by 9 m garden.
steps: Draw the shape and label every side you are given.
Work out any missing side lengths first.
Either split into rectangles and add, or take a big rectangle and subtract the missing piece.
Add or subtract the areas to get the total.

methods.title: How to estimate the area of an irregular shape
methods.example: A square has an area of 36 cm². What is the length of each side, and what is its perimeter?
steps: Place the shape on a grid of squares of known size.
Count all the whole squares fully inside the shape.
Count each part square that is half-or-more as one whole.
Add the whole and part squares for your estimate.

methods.title: Area of a triangle
methods.example: A triangle has a base of 8 cm and a height of 6 cm. Find its area.
steps: Solution: Area = ½ × base × height.
First multiply the base and height: 8 × 6 = 48.
Then take half: 48 ÷ 2 = 24 cm².
The height must be the straight distance from the base to the top point, not a slanting side.

methods.title: Working backwards to a missing side
methods.example: A rectangular field has an area of 84 m² and a width of 7 m. Find its length and perimeter.
steps: Solution: Since area = length × width, we have 84 = length × 7, so length = 84 ÷ 7 = 12 m.
Now the perimeter: P = 2 × (12 + 7) = 2 × 19 = 38 m.
Check the working backwards to a missing side result carefully.

methods.title: Area of a compound shape
methods.example: An L-shaped room is a large rectangle 10 cm by 6 cm with a 4 cm by 3 cm corner removed. Find its area.
steps: Solution: Use the subtraction method.
The big rectangle is 10 × 6 = 60 cm².
The removed corner is 4 × 3 = 12 cm².
So the area is 60 − 12 = 48 cm².

workedExamples.title: Perimeter of a rectangle
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular garden is 12 m long and 9 m wide. Find its perimeter.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: We are going around the edge, so this is perimeter. P = 2 × (length + width) = 2 × (12 + 9) = 2 × 21 = 42 m. So 42 metres of fencing would go all the way around.

workedExamples.title: Area of a rectangle
workedExamples.prompt: Find the area of the same 12 m by 9 m garden.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: We are covering the inside, so this is area. A = length × width = 12 × 9 = 108 m². Notice the answer is in square metres because area covers a surface.

workedExamples.title: Square, working backwards
workedExamples.prompt: A square has an area of 36 cm². What is the length of each side, and what is its perimeter?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For a square, area = side × side. We need the number that multiplies by itself to give 36. Since 6 × 6 = 36, the side is 6 cm. The perimeter is then 4 × 6 = 24 cm.

workedExamples.title: Area of a triangle
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has a base of 8 cm and a height of 6 cm. Find its area.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Area = ½ × base × height. First multiply the base and height: 8 × 6 = 48. Then take half: 48 ÷ 2 = 24 cm². The height must be the straight distance from the base to the top point, not a slanting side.

workedExamples.title: Working backwards to a missing side
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular field has an area of 84 m² and a width of 7 m. Find its length and perimeter.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Since area = length × width, we have 84 = length × 7, so length = 84 ÷ 7 = 12 m. Now the perimeter: P = 2 × (12 + 7) = 2 × 19 = 38 m.

workedExamples.title: Area of a compound shape
workedExamples.prompt: An L-shaped room is a large rectangle 10 cm by 6 cm with a 4 cm by 3 cm corner removed. Find its area.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use the subtraction method. The big rectangle is 10 × 6 = 60 cm². The removed corner is 4 × 3 = 12 cm². So the area is 60 − 12 = 48 cm².

workedExamples.title: A cost problem
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular room is 5 m by 4 m. Carpet costs 25 shillings per square metre. How much will it cost to carpet the room?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Carpet covers the floor, so we need area: A = 5 × 4 = 20 m². The cost is 20 × 25 = 500 shillings.

workedExamples.title: An optimisation problem
workedExamples.prompt: You have 24 m of fencing to make a rectangular pen. What dimensions give the largest area?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For a fixed perimeter, a square gives the maximum area. The four equal sides come from 24 ÷ 4 = 6 m. So a 6 m by 6 m square gives the largest area, which is 6 × 6 = 36 m².

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: A square tile has an area of 25 cm². Find the length of one side.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Convert: a) 2 m² into cm², b) 4.85 m into cm.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: An L-shaped figure is a 10 cm by 6 cm rectangle with a 3 cm by 2 cm corner removed. Find its area.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: You have 40 m of fencing to make a rectangular pen. What dimensions give the largest area, and what is that area?

activities.title: Measure Your Own Room
activities.materials: a tape measure or a metre ruler, paper and a pencil.
steps: Measure the length and width of a room or a mat in your home, in metres.
Work out the area using length × width, and write it with the unit m².
Work out the perimeter using 2 × (length + width), and write it with the unit m.
Imagine carpet costs 25 shillings per square metre. How much would it cost to carpet your room?
Keep your measurements – you can compare different rooms and see which has the largest area.

activities.title: Squared-Paper Shapes
activities.materials: squared (grid) paper, a ruler and coloured pencils.
steps: On squared paper, draw three different rectangles that each have a perimeter of 24 cm.
Count the squares inside each rectangle to find its area.
Which rectangle has the largest area? Which has the smallest?
Write down what you notice about the shape with the largest area.
This activity shows that shapes with the same perimeter can have very different areas.

activities.title: The Estimation Game
activities.materials: squared paper, a pencil and a few flat objects (a leaf, your hand, a spoon).
steps: Place an object on the squared paper and draw around its outline.
First estimate the area by eye – write down your guess.
Now count the whole squares inside, then the part squares that are half-or-more.
Compare your count with your first guess. How close were you? Try three objects and see if your estimates improve.

activities.title: Garden Designer Investigation
activities.materials: squared paper, a ruler and coloured pencils.
steps: Design an L-shaped garden on squared paper, letting each square stand for 1 metre.
Split your L-shape into two rectangles and find the area of each, then add them.
Now check your answer using the subtraction method (big rectangle minus the missing corner).
Work out the perimeter by carefully adding every outside edge – do not miss any sides!

activities.title: Fencing and Flooring Challenge (Family Task)
activities.materials: your family, a tape measure and a calculator or paper.
steps: Choose a real space at home or outside, such as a room or a small yard.
Measure it and work out both the area and the perimeter together.
Decide: for new flooring, do you need area or perimeter? For a fence or border, which do you need?
Make up sensible prices per metre and per square metre, and work out the total cost as a family.

activities.title: Triangle Halves
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, scissors and coloured pencils.
steps: Draw a rectangle and work out its area with length × width.
Draw a straight line from one corner to the opposite corner, then cut along it.
You now have two matching triangles. Check that each triangle is exactly half the rectangle's area.
Explain in your own words why the area of a triangle is ½ × base × height.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand the difference between area and perimeter best? What was the trickiest part? Did your estimates get better with practice? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: To find the perimeter of any shape
rules.text: To find the perimeter of any shape, you simply add up the lengths of all its sides. That rule never fails, and it works for every shape, no matter how strange. For common shapes, though, we have quicker methods. For a rectangle, opposite sides are equal, so the perimeter is length + width + length + width. We write this neatly as Perimeter = 2 × (length + width). For a square, all four sides are equal, so Perimeter = 4 × side length.

rules.title: Let us work an example. Amina marks
rules.text: Let us work an example. Amina marks out a rectangular vegetable plot that is 5 m long and 3 m wide. Using the rule, Perimeter = 2 × (5 + 3) = 2 × 8 = 16 m. So she needs 16 metres of fencing to go all the way around. Now a square example: a square prayer mat has sides of 4 cm on a small model. Perimeter = 4 × 4 = 16 cm. Notice both answers came out as 16, but one is metres of fence and the other is centimetres of edge – always keep the unit attached to the number.

rules.title: Why does length × width give the
rules.text: Why does length × width give the area? Imagine covering a rectangle with 1 cm squares. If the rectangle is 6 cm long and 4 cm wide, you can fit 6 squares along each row, and there are 4 rows. That is 6 × 4 = 24 little squares, so the area is 24 cm². Counting rows of squares is exactly what multiplication does quickly for us. This is why the rule for a rectangle is Area = length × width.

rules.title: The key skill for compound shapes is
rules.text: The key skill for compound shapes is finding those missing side lengths. Here is the rule that never fails: opposite sides of the whole shape must balance. The lengths sticking up on one side must add to the same total as the tall side opposite them, and the same for the horizontal sides. Always draw the shape, label every length you are given, and then reason out the missing ones before you add or multiply. Rushing straight to the numbers is what causes mistakes here.

rules.title: Here is a practical reason this matters
rules.text: Here is a practical reason this matters. Idris measures a poster as 80 cm by 60 cm and wants its area in square metres. He can first convert the sides: 80 cm = 0.8 m and 60 cm = 0.6 m, then multiply: 0.8 × 0.6 = 0.48 m². Doing the conversion before multiplying often keeps the numbers tidy. The golden rule is simple: before you calculate area or perimeter, make sure all your measurements are in the same unit. Mixing metres and centimetres in one calculation is a certain path to a wrong answer.

terms: The distance around the outside edge of a shape
The amount of flat space inside a shape
A square one unit wide, used to measure area
The bottom side of a triangle / the straight distance up from it
A shape made from two or more simple shapes
A shape with crooked or curved sides
A rule written with numbers and symbols
commonMistakes: Ask: around the edge or covering inside?
cm, m for perimeter; cm², m² for area
Using a slanting side as height
Measure straight up from the base
Missing a side in a compound shape
Draw, label, and tick each side

realProblems.prompt: A house has a main room 8 m by 5 m and a kitchen extension 4 m by 3 m joined to it. Find the total floor area.
realProblems.answer: Area = 6 × 2.5 = 15 m²; 15 ÷ 8 = 1.875, so round up to 2 tins.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The square is 5 cm by 5 cm with area 25 cm²; the rectangle is 6 × 4 = 24 cm²; the square is larger by 1 cm².

questions.question: What does “Perimeter” mean?
options: The distance around the outside edge of a shape
The amount of flat space inside a shape
A square one unit wide, used to measure area
The bottom side of a triangle / the straight distance up from it
questions.answer: The distance around the outside edge of a shape
questions.hint: Use the Unit 14 Math Words & Symbols reference.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is written for self-paced learning. That means you set the speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto your own paper and work it through yourself, and only move on once the idea feels solid. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you more practice, to explain a step in a fresh way, or to check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels difficult.

I can keep a pencil, a ruler and some squared paper beside you as you work. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the shapes, label the sides, and say each formula out loud as you use it. When you make a mistake, do not worry; a mistake is simply a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Every skill in this unit becomes automatic with a little honest practice, and the confidence you build here will carry you into area of triangles, surface area and volume in the years ahead.
I can explain the difference between perimeter (distance around) and area (space inside).
I can measure and calculate the perimeter of rectangles, squares, triangles and irregular shapes.
I can calculate the area of rectangles and squares using length × width.
I can calculate the area of a triangle using ½ × base × height.
I can find the area and perimeter of compound shapes by splitting or subtracting rectangles.
I can estimate the area of an irregular shape by counting whole and part squares.
